title: Nature
abstract: Nature, Published online: 05 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01610-9 European governments are choosing European digital tools — and some universities and researchers are doing the same.

title: See a helicopter destined for Mars and a spectacular flowery frame for the Milky Way — May's best science images
abstract: Nature, Published online: 05 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01609-2 The month’s sharpest science shots, selected by Nature’s photo team.

title: Electric vehicles cut pollution in China – and prevent 260,000 premature deaths
abstract: Nature, Published online: 05 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01781-5 Having fewer fossil-fuel powered cars on the road is reducing some pollutants, but not others.

title: Author Correction: Physiology and immunology of a pig-to-human decedent kidney xenotransplant
abstract: Nature, Published online: 04 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10694-2 Author Correction: Physiology and immunology of a pig-to-human decedent kidney xenotransplant

title: Device could sniff out fusion reactors secretly making material for a nuclear bomb
abstract: Nature, Published online: 04 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01764-6 Antineutrinos made alongside the production of weapons-grade plutonium could be spotted with existing technology.

title: Babies’ birth weight improves with help of payments to parents
abstract: Nature, Published online: 04 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01765-5 Interventions that included home visits and cash transfers led to more strapping infants in an Indian state.

title: This mysterious lung disease affects millions of people — but a drug tested in mice shows promise
abstract: Nature, Published online: 04 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01789-x The therapy activates a protein that could stop pulmonary fibrosis from progressing.

title: Science with military applications is cited more than civilian-only research
abstract: Nature, Published online: 04 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01770-8 Researchers analysed the scientific impact of dual-use research in US patent records and bibliometric databases.

title: What’s behind China’s historically high counts of corresponding authors?
abstract: Nature, Published online: 04 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01623-4 From 2016 to 2020, almost one-third of papers by authors in China had multiple corresponding authors — but policy reforms are expected to have dampened the trend.

title: Editorial Expression of Concern: Functional proteomic identification of DNA replication proteins by induced proteolysis in vivo
abstract: Nature, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10727-w Editorial Expression of Concern: Functional proteomic identification of DNA replication proteins by induced proteolysis in vivo

title: AI in science recruitment: friend or foe? Join our free webinar
abstract: Nature, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01774-4 Discover the strengths, limitations and risks associated with using artificial intelligence in your science job applications.

title: Your phone can use tiny skin-colour changes to measure your heart rate
abstract: Nature, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01793-1 Passive heart-rate monitoring during regular phone use could provide early warning of health issues — plus, testing Richard Feynman’s solution to the ‘restaurant dilemma’ problem.

title: Reply to: The size of tropical vegetation gross primary production
abstract: Nature, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10561-0 Reply to: The size of tropical vegetation gross primary production

title: The size of tropical vegetation gross primary production
abstract: Nature, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10562-z The size of tropical vegetation gross primary production

title: A natural depsipeptide antibiotic binds the E-site of the bacterial ribosome
abstract: Nature, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10589-2 Improved fractionation strategies can identify antibiotics with previously unseen scaffolds and mechanisms, exemplified by manikomycin from Streptomyces rimosus, which acts by targeting the E-site of the bacterial large ribosomal subunit.

title: Earth’s east–west albedo symmetry
abstract: Nature, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10624-2 Analysis of a 25-year satellite record shows that Earth has a persistent east–west albedo symmetry split at 27° E, with clear-sky albedo, cloud radiative effect and open-ocean fraction exhibiting a triple symmetry around this meridian.

title: Relativistic electron acceleration at the bow shock of Jupiter and beyond
abstract: Nature, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10473-z NASA’s Juno observations show relativistic electron acceleration at the bow shock of Jupiter and establish a universal scaling law linking shock size to maximum cosmic ray energy.

title: Acquired genetic and cell-state changes in IDH-mutant glioma progression
abstract: Nature, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10612-6 Longitudinal transcriptomic, chromatin and genomic analyses of the two types of IDH-mutant glioma reveal in detail how disease progression is influenced by interdependent genetic, epigenetic and microenvironmental factors.

title: Mining triggers extensive additional deforestation in sub-Saharan Africa
abstract: Nature, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10551-2 Extensive mining activity leads to large-scale deforestation of dense tropical forests across sub-Saharan Africa, driven particularly by key energy transition minerals such as cobalt.

title: Cold-induced peptide signalling secures pollen resilience and crop yield
abstract: Nature, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10603-7 A small-peptide signalling axis involving RGF family members controls resilience to cold stress in tomatoes and rice; modulating this pathway prevents cold-induced yield losses.

title: Nature Reviews Biodiversity
abstract: Nature Reviews Biodiversity, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s44358-026-00162-0 The loss of genetic diversity is a major threat to population resilience, posing a challenge for conservation efforts. This Review highlights how the integration of large-scale biodiversity monitoring datasets and population genetics principles can empower biodiversity partners across governance levels to make effective conservation decisions.

title: Author Correction: The fate of mountain biodiversity in a warming world
abstract: Nature Reviews Biodiversity, Published online: 02 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s44358-026-00172-y Author Correction: The fate of mountain biodiversity in a warming world

title: The art of the proposal
abstract: Nature Reviews Biodiversity, Published online: 01 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s44358-026-00174-w Nature Reviews Biodiversity mainly publishes commissioned articles, but we also welcome proposals. Understanding what the editors look for in a proposal is the first step towards preparing a successful pitch.

title: The principles underpinning the evolution of the brain
abstract: Nature Reviews Biodiversity, Published online: 27 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s44358-026-00171-z The principles underpinning the evolution of the brain

title: The fate of mountain biodiversity in a warming world
abstract: Nature Reviews Biodiversity, Published online: 25 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s44358-026-00167-9 Species living on mountains are thought to be especially vulnerable to ongoing climate change. This Review explores past and present responses of mountaintop species to climate warming to help to understand mountain species’ vulnerabilities and resilience to future warming.

title: Running the numbers on biodiversity change
abstract: Nature Reviews Biodiversity, Published online: 20 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s44358-026-00163-z Predictive modelling and attribution science have advanced further in climate science than in biodiversity science, despite a history of strong quantitative approaches in ecology. Efforts to attribute biodiversity loss to anthropogenic drivers are escalating, given the urgency of providing evidence of biodiversity loss and identifying effective interventions.

title: Biodiversity conservation in the era of environmental instability
abstract: Nature Reviews Biodiversity, Published online: 19 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s44358-026-00168-8 Biodiversity conservation in the era of environmental instability

title: Updating species representation targets for protected and conserved area planning
abstract: Nature Reviews Biodiversity, Published online: 19 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s44358-026-00169-7 Current standards for assessing species representation in protected areas are outdated. We propose raising the minimum representation target to 30% of a species range, lowering the threshold for assigning a 100% representation target to species with ranges below 200 km2, and adopting dynamic targets that evolve with global conservation commitments.

title: Nature Ecology & Evolution
abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 05 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03101-6 A study that couples ecological–economic scenarios with a machine-learned replica of how nations’ credit ratings are determined shows that even partial losses of just three ecosystem services could materially raise the cost of government borrowing.

title: Latitudinal patterns and environmental correlates of plant root exudation
abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 05 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03103-4 From an assembled dataset of plant root exudation rate, root traits and environmental covariates, this study shows that the rate of carbon exuded by roots generally increases from high to low latitudes, and soil phosphorus deficiency is the most important influencing factor.

title: Biodiversity loss will decrease the future creditworthiness of nations
abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 05 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03081-7 Scenario modelling reveals how sovereign credit ratings and the cost of borrowing across 23 nations would change under partial collapses in timber production, wild pollination services and marine fisheries.

title: Different planning scales for renewable expansion yield distinct biodiversity trade-offs in China
abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03097-z In this study, China’s solar and wind expansion through to 2060 is projected under national versus provincial planning scenarios, which represent different centralization levels. Modelling shows that each pathway results in distinct trade-offs with biodiversity, which demonstrates the need for integrated, multiscale siting to align climate and biodiversity conservation goals.

title: Twentieth-century insect declines and partial recoveries driven by changes in land use and climate
abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 02 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03083-5 Continuous 90-year trends of butterflies and deadwood-dependent beetles show clear mid-century declines and only partial recovery of these taxa. The declines were clearly related to agricultural intensification, and climate change and more biodiversity-friendly management helped in the recovery and stabilization — although butterflies are still far from their original diversity.

title: Alignment-free integration of single-nucleus ATAC-seq across species with sPYce
abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 02 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03087-1 This paper presents sPYce, a method that integrates single-nucleus ATAC-seq data across species, allowing for comparison of gene regulatory evolution across species.

title: Gene body methylation suppresses intragenic transcription and permits epigenetic inheritance in a cnidarian
abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 02 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03090-6 Experiments in the cnidarian Nematostella vectensis show a role of gene body methylation in transposable element suppression and that epigenetic inheritance is constrained by chromatin context and transcriptional activity.

title: Ninety-year trends reveal sharpest insect declines in the mid-twentieth century
abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 02 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03074-6 Assessing the magnitude and drivers of recent biodiversity losses requires informative, long-term baseline data. Here the authors reconstruct the temporal trajectories of butterflies and saproxylic beetles in Switzerland from 1930 to 2021, shedding light on the role of climate and land-use change.

title: A hidden pollutant is changing how the world's forests breathe
abstract: A massive global analysis found that nitrogen pollution can either speed up or dramatically slow the natural "breathing" of forest soils, depending on the ecosystem's condition. The results reveal hidden tipping points that could affect how forests store carbon and cope with climate change.

title: This blood-feeding fly sacrifices its sight after finding a host
abstract: Deer keds rely on flight and vision to find a host, but everything changes once they land. After shedding their wings forever, these parasites reduce the activity of key vision-related genes by about half. Scientists believe they are effectively trading sharp eyesight for extra energy that can be used for feeding and reproduction.

title: Scientists say evolution may work differently than we thought
abstract: A major research study is challenging one of evolution’s most influential ideas: that most genetic changes that become permanent are essentially neutral. Researchers at the University of Michigan found that beneficial mutations are actually far more common than scientists have long assumed. The puzzle is that these advantageous mutations rarely spread through entire populations. Their answer? Nature keeps changing the rules.

title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
abstract: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

title: Plant believed extinct for 60 years suddenly reappears
abstract: A random photo snapped in the Australian outback has led to the rediscovery of a plant thought extinct for nearly 60 years — proving that ordinary people with smartphones are quietly transforming science. After bird bander Aaron Bean uploaded pictures of a strange shrub to iNaturalist, botanist Anthony Bean immediately recognized it as Ptilotus senarius, a rare species missing since 1967.

title: Arctic Ocean passed a tipping point and scientists say it may never recover
abstract: The Arctic Ocean may have crossed a dangerous tipping point. Scientists say the rapid disappearance of sea ice is triggering a hidden chemical shift that is stripping the ocean of nitrate — a nutrient essential for the tiny plankton that support Arctic life. As nitrate levels plunge, the entire food web could feel the impact, from fish and seabirds to whales and polar ecosystems.

title: Humanity has already exceeded Earth’s limits, study warns
abstract: Humanity may already be living far beyond what Earth can sustainably support, according to a sweeping new study analyzing more than 200 years of population and environmental data. Researchers found that while population growth once fueled innovation and expansion, the trend shifted decades ago as the planet’s resources became increasingly strained.

title: Lost for 150,000 years: Rainforest discovery upends human history
abstract: For decades, scientists believed ancient humans avoided dense rainforests, treating them as nearly impossible environments for early survival. But a groundbreaking discovery in West Africa is rewriting that story. Researchers uncovered evidence that humans were living deep within rainforest environments in present-day Côte d'Ivoire around 150,000 years ago — far earlier than anyone thought possible.
